coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots enhanced interfacial electron storage and transfer of iron phosphide for productively photocatalytic water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Si, Honglin
Li, Zenan
Zhang, Tianyang
Fan, Zhenglong
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Iron phosphide (FeP), as a low-cost and high-performance phosphide, not only has good stability but also possesses broad optical absorption, which can be used as a visible light or even near-infrared photocatalyst. However, their limited photocatalytic performance arises from the rapid charge recombination and a scarcity of active sites. Here, FeP was modified by introducing carbon dots (CDs), and the photocatalytic performance for H2 production of CDs/FeP reached 33.85 μmol/h/g, which improved significantly compared to that of the pristine FeP (6.65 μmol/h/g). The catalytic reaction goes through a 4-electron passway, which generates O2 and H2 simultaneously. CDs can not merely accelerate the interfacial charge delivery rate, but also can rapidly store photogenerated electrons, reducing the recombination of electrons and holes to improve the photocatalytic performance of FeP. This work provides a new perspective on enhancing the photocatalytic water activity of phosphides by improving electron transfer at the phosphide interface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
FeP
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
Transient photovoltage

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20220028 ), National Key R&D Program of China ( 2020YFA0406104 , 2020YFA0406101 ), National MCF Energy R&D Program of China ( 2018YFE0306105 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52271223 , ‬ 52272043 , 52202107 , 52201269 , 51821002 ), The Science and Technology Development Fund, Macau SAR (0009/2022/ITP), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project , and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image, (b) HAADF-STEM and the corresponding element mapping images of (c) C, (d) Fe and (e) P, (f) TEM image, and (g) HRTEM image of 4% CDs/FeP.
(a) FTIR spectra of CDs and 4 % CDs/FeP. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s for 4 % CDs/FeP and CDs; (c) Fe 2p and (d) P 2p for FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of 4% CDs/FeP and FeP. (b) CV curve of 4% CDs/FeP. (c) Mott-Schottky plots of FeP. (d) Band structure diagram of 4% CDs/FeP.

(a) Time courses of photocatalytic H2 evolution over 4 % CDs/FeP. (b) H2 evolution of CDs/FeP with different ratios of CDs. (c) Stability test of photocatalytic ability for water splitting over 4 % CDs/FeP. (d) The AQE spectrum under the light of 365, 420, 500, 590, and 660 nm with the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of 4 % CDs/FeP.

(a) EIS curves of 4 % CDs/FeP and FeP. (b) Photocurrent curves of 4 % CDs/FeP and FeP. (c) Electron transfer number curves of 4 % CDs/FeP under N2 saturation (0.1 M Na2SO4) from dark to light on. (d) Rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) voltammogram under N2 saturation (0.1 M phosphate buffer solution). A potential of −0.23 V versus Ag/AgCl was applied to the ring electrode to detect O2.

(a) TPV curves of 4% CDs/FeP and FeP. (b) Electron attenuation constants (τ) of 4% CDs/FeP and FeP. (c) Maximum charge extraction efficiency (A) of 4% CDs/FeP and FeP under charge extraction process. (d) Maximum transfer rate of charge (tmax) of 4% CDs/FeP and FeP.
Schematic diagram of charge transfer and photocatalytic hydrogen production in the CDs/FeP under illumination.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots enhanced interfacial electron storage and transfer of iron phosphide for productively photocatalytic water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering (MIMSE), MUST-SUDA Joint Research Center for Advanced Functional Materials, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa 999078, Macau

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering (MIMSE)
MUST-SUDA Joint Research Center for Advanced Functional Materials
Macau University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering (MIMSE), MUST-SUDA Joint Research Center for Advanced Functional Materials, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa 999078, Macau

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Well-designed CDs modified FeP shows the photocatalytic performance for H2 production reached 33.85 μmol/h/g, which improved significantly compared to that of the pristine FeP (6.65 μmol/h/g). The presence of CDs in the system enhances the electron sink effect, facilitating the efficient transfer of photogenerated electrons from FeP to the CDs. This electron transfer process subsequently drives the surface photoreduction of H+ on the CDs surface, leading to the generation of H2.

Iron phosphide (FeP), as a low-cost and high-performance phosphide, not only has good stability but also possesses broad optical absorption, which can be used as a visible light or even near-infrared photocatalyst. However, their limited photocatalytic performance arises from the rapid charge recombination and a scarcity of active sites. Here, FeP was modified by introducing carbon dots (CDs), and the photocatalytic performance for H2 production of CDs/FeP reached 33.85 μmol/h/g, which improved significantly compared to that of the pristine FeP (6.65 μmol/h/g). The catalytic reaction goes through a 4-electron passway, which generates O2 and H2 simultaneously. CDs can not merely accelerate the interfacial charge delivery rate, but also can rapidly store photogenerated electrons, reducing the recombination of electrons and holes to improve the photocatalytic performance of FeP. This work provides a new perspective on enhancing the photocatalytic water activity of phosphides by improving electron transfer at the phosphide interface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic water splitting represents a pioneering and eco-friendly catalytic technology that holds profound importance within the prevailing backdrop of escalating energy and environmental challenges [1,2]. This advanced approach enables the efficient utilization of solar energy to drive the splitting of water molecules, thereby generating clean hydrogen fuel and offering a potential solution to the pressing energy crisis [3,4]. Presently, within the realm of photocatalysis, the majority of catalysts demonstrating high efficiency necessitate the inclusion of precious metals such as Pt as co-catalysts [5–9]. Due to the limited reserves of these precious metals on Earth, their widespread application is hindered by their lack of economic feasibility [10–16]. Transition metal phosphides such as iron phosphide (FeP) with excellent electrical conductivity and stability are considered as efficient H2 generation co-catalysts [17,18]. Zheng’s group reported FeP/g-C3N4 nanodots, for the boosted H2 evolution compared to the pristine g-C3N4 under visible light [19]. Sun et al. reported a heterojunction photocatalyst consisting of FeP nanoparticles/CdS nanosheets for excellent H2 production [20]. At the present stage, FeP is mainly used as a co-catalyst to assist in photocatalytic hydrogen production. For the photocatalyst design, the conversion of cocatalysts into efficient photocatalysts is a new way for the design and manufacture of next-generation photocatalysts.

Carbon dots (CDs) referred to zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with the diameter of less than 10 nm [21,22], which have excellent photoelectric properties, the advantages of low toxicity and good biocompatibility [23,24]. In comparison to conventional metal quantum dots, such as CdSe, WO3, SnO2 and CdS, CDs exhibit notable advantages in terms of both photostability and environmental compatibility [25–27]. This stark contrast with metal-based quantum dots makes CDs a desirable alternative, particularly in applications where photostability and eco-friendliness are critical considerations [28,29]. Commonly, CDs exhibit a diverse range of optical attributes, spanning from UV to IR photoluminescence. Furthermore, CDs can serve as acceptors for electrons or holes and can also generate H+ or electrons through photo-stimulation [30]. These compelling advantages empower CDs to enhance light absorption and stabilize the hole-electron pairs.

In this work, the design and fabrication of CDs/FeP photocatalysts using ferric trichloride (FeCl3) as precursor were reported. The obtained CDs/FeP nanoparticles exhibit the morphology of an urch-like structure and were verified as a promising photocatalyst in the photolysis of ultrapure water with a H2 production of 33.85 μmol/h/g. By employing transient photovoltage (TPV) assessments, the interfacial electron transfer kinetics of the CDs/FeP catalyst were explored. This research endeavours to shed light on the unique characteristics and potential applications of FeP-based photocatalysts modified with CDs, offering insights into novel avenues for the design and development of efficient and sustainable photocatalytic systems.

FeCl3·6H2O (99 %) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Na2SO4 (99.9 %) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co., Ltd. NaH2PO3 (99 %) Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Graphite rod (99.99 %) was purchased from Shanghai Carbon Co., Ltd. Nafion perfluorinated resin solution (5 wt%) was purchased from Adamas-Betas. All other solvents and reagents were obtained from commercial sources and used without further purification unless otherwise noted. The water used in all experiments was obtained from a Millipore system (18.25 MΩ cm).

Two graphite rods are placed in ultrapure water as working electrodes and electrolyzed at 30 V, 25℃. The graphite rod is gradually corroded, and the solution gradually turns brown. The brown solution containing CDs is then filtered and freeze-dried to produce CDs powder [31].

Scheme S1 exhibits the synthesis of 4 % CDs/FeP. The catalyst precursor was synthesized by hydrothermal method. First, weigh 0.8 g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.48 g Na2SO4 in a beaker containing 70 mL aqueous solution of carbon quantum dots (0, 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 mg) and stir for 15 min. Next, the stirred solution is transferred to a 100 mL PTFE hydrothermal kettle and heated at 120 °C for 6 h. Finally, the product was centrifuged at a rotational speed of 12000 rpm for 3 min, repeatedly washed with ultrapure water 3 times, and then washed 3 times with absolute ethanol, and the precipitate was dried in vacuo at 70 °C after the last centrifugation to obtain the CDs/FeP precursor. Then, weigh 0.1 g precursor and 1 g sodium hypophosphite and lie on both sides of the porcelain boat, place the porcelain boat with sodium hypophosphite on one side close to the inlet of the tubular furnace, heat it to 300 °C under saturated N2 atmosphere and maintain it for 2 h, so that the N2 gas flow drives the phosphine gas generated by the thermal decomposition of sodium hypophosphite and reacts with the iron phosphide precursor. The resulting product was then washed with water and ethanol 3 times and dried under vacuum conditions at 70 °C to give the CDs/FeP composites (FeP, 2 % CDs/FeP, 4 % CDs/FeP, 6 % CDs/FeP, 8 % CDs/FeP and 10 % CDs/FeP).

Fig. 1 a is the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of the 4 % CDs/FeP nanocomposites. From Fig. 1a, it can be observed that the synthesized 4 % CDs/FeP nanocomposites are uniform in size and have an average diameter of about 2 μm (Fig. S3, Supporting Information) with an urchin-like structure. The urchin-like structure has a smooth surface, and the whiskers extend to the outside of the sphere and are evenly distributed on the spherical surface. The production of this morphology is related to unique synthesis method and the regulatory effects of CDs [32]. Compared with the traditional spherical structure, the microstructure of whiskers has the advantage of having a large specific surface area (SSA) and results in more photocatalytic active sites on its surface, which has great significance for the improvement of HER [33,34].

Fig. 1b–e show the high-angle annular dark field micrograph and elemental mapping images of 4 % CDs/FeP, which reveal that Fe, P, and C elements are evenly distributed in the nanoparticles. The TEM image of 4 % CDs/FeP in Fig. 1f and the HRTEM image in Fig. 1g show the lattice fringes of the nanoparticle. The lattice spacings of 0.296 and 0.258 nm correspond to the (110) and (200) crystal planes of FeP. In addition, the lattice spacing of CDs (0.21 nm) along with FeP (0.262 nm) are exhibited in Fig. S4, which fits the (100) spacing of graphite carbon and (200) spacing of FeP [35,36]. Fig. S5 is the XRD diffraction patterns of CDs, FeP, and 4 % CDs/FeP. Four strong peaks at 32.8°, 46.5°, 47.2°, and 48.4° and five weak peaks at 35.5°, 37.2°, 50.5°, 56.2°, and 59.6° can be identified in both FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP. Compared the measured XRD pattern with JCPDS-No. 78–1443, the peaks of FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP at 32.8°, 35.5°, 37.2°, 46.5°, 47.2°, 48.4°, 50.5°, 56.2°, and 59.6° match the (011), (102), (111), (112), (202), (211), (103), (212) and (020) crystal planes of FeP orthogonal phase respectively. The XRD pattern of CDs exhibits two characteristic peaks at 20.7° and 26.5°, which correspond to a short-range order in stacked graphene layer and graphene phase [37]. The perfect symmetry between the XRD patterns of FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP, thoroughly validates the lattice integrity of 4 % CDs/FeP after the incorporation of CDs.

Fig. 2 a shows the FTIR spectra of 4 % CDs/FeP compared with FeP to exhibit more detailed information on the nanoparticle. The peaks from 3300 to 3470 cm−1 indicate the existence of OH structure while the peaks of 2851 and 2921 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of CH. Besides, the absorption peak of 1714 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO and CC respectively. The peak of 1041 cm−1 and 863 cm−1 indicates the CO structure [38]. And for 4 % CDs/FeP, a peak of 545 cm−1 appears, which can be ascribed to Fe-P structure [39]. All the typical peaks of CDs could match that of the 4 % CDs/FeP, which confirm the existence of CDs in the synthesized catalyst.

Fig. S6 exhibits the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra for FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP, which show the peaks of Fe, P, C, and O elements. The existence of C and O signals is owing to carbon contamination and surface oxidation [40]. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) exhibits CO bond (288.9 eV), CH bond (285.8 eV), and CC bond (284.8 eV) in 4 % CDs/FeP and CDs, which indicates the existence of CDs in 4 % CDs/FeP [41]. By comparison, the binding energy for C 1s in 4 % CDs/FeP exhibits negative shifts. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 2c) can be deconvoluted into eight subpeaks, including two Fe-P peaks (707.3/720.4 eV), three FeO peaks (709.5/712.5 and 725.6 eV) and two satellite peaks (715.9 and 729.3 eV) of the Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 core level [42]. The P 2p spectrum exhibits three subpeaks, including two P-Fe peaks (129.4 and 130.4 eV) belonging to the P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 orbitals, and one PO4 3- peak (134.1 eV) [40,43]. These above XPS results provide evidence for the presence of both metallic iron phosphide (FeP) and oxidized iron species on the surface of FeP. This coexistence can be attributed to the unavoidable surface oxidation that occurs when FeP is exposed to ambient air, as previously demonstrated in relevant studies [43–46]. As noted by the dashed line (in Fig. 2b and d), the binding energy of the Fe-O bonds for 4 % CDs/FeP has a slight shift by +0.14 eV, which can be attributed to the formation of the Fe-O-C association. While the peak area of the P-Fe bond shows a slight increase after the addition of CDs into FeP which can be ascribed to the existence of CDs in the outer region of FeP. The observed positive shifts in the binding energies of P-Fe and Fe-P species along with the negative shifts of C provide compelling evidence for electron redistribution phenomena between CDs and FeP. These shifts imply that there is a transfer of electron density between CDs and FeP, leading to alterations in the electronic structure and bonding interactions at the interface between the two components. As the electrons from FeP transfer to CDs, electrons and holes are accumulated on CDs and FeP respectively [37,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and energy level structure
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a is the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy of 4 % CDs/FeP and FeP, which shows the optical properties of the catalysts. The light-harvesting capacity at the wavelength between 550 nm and 800 nm becomes higher after the modification of CDs, proving that CDs improve the ability of photo-absorption. Cycle voltammetry (CV) and the Mott-Schottky test were carried out to study the conduction band (CB) and valance bond (VB) position of 4 % CDs/FeP. Fig. 3b shows the CV curve of 4 % CDs/FeP, which indicates that the CB and VB positions of 4 % CDs/FeP are −0.088 V and 1.579 V (vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE)). The CV curve was tested by a three-electrode system using a glass carbon electrode as the working electrode, Ag/Ag+ electrode as the reference electrode, and a graphite rod as the counter electrode. The band gap (Eg) of 4 % CDs/FeP is calculated to be 1.667 according to the formula Eg = EVB − ECB. Detailed calculation of CV is provided in S1, Supporting Information.

The determination of the flat band potential (Efb) corresponding to the Fermi level was carried out through the analysis of Mott-Schottky plots in Fig. 3c. In the present study, the Efb of the 4 %CDs/FeP photocatalyst is found to be 0.025 V vs. RHE. The Mott-Schottky plots of FeP reveal positive values, indicating its characteristic n-type semiconductor properties. In n-type semiconductors, the conduction band position (ECB) is typically found to be negatively shifted by approximately 0.1 V or 0.2 V relative to the flat band potential (Efb) value. Consequently, based on this relationship, the ECB of FeP can be calculated to be −0.072 V vs. RHE, which matches the ECB determined by CV tests (−0.088 V vs. RHE)[48,49]. Fig. 3d shows the band structure of 4 % CDs/FeP based on the analysis above. The band structure of 4 % CDs/FeP is compared with the potentials of WOR and HER and proves that 4 % CDs/FeP meets the condition of photocatalytic overall water splitting.

The photocatalytic performance of different CDs/FeP catalysts was measured through a multichannel photocatalytic reaction system and conducted in ultrapure water under atmospheric pressure in a normal air atmosphere. Fig. 4 a exhibits the photoinduced water splitting of H2 evolution process for 44 h catalysed by 4 % CDs/FeP. During the first eight h, when the system was set in a dark environment, no H2 production was detected, which means no dark reaction happened during the process. After the lambs were turned on, H2 and O2 were generated constantly for 36 h. Additionally, the catalyst was also tested for H2O2, the results show that FeP/CDs catalyst cannot produce H2O2. To explore the effect of CDs on FeP, the H2 evolution rate was tested for different addition of CDs. Fig. 4b shows that 4 % CDs/FeP has the best H2 evolution rate among various percentages of CDs (0, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 0.10) which is 33.85 μmol/h/g. The addition of CDs has an obvious improvement in the photocatalytic ability of FeP. However, excess addition of CDs (after 4 %) inhabits the H2 generation rate suggesting that CDs deactivate the photocatalytic ability of FeP/CDs catalyst after a certain amount.

To test the catalytic stability of 4 % CDs/FeP, the cyclic stability tests for 4 % CDs/FeP were carried out and shown in Fig. 4c. The result shows that 4 % CDs/FeP can maintain its photocatalytic ability after five cycles. Fig. S7 shows the XPS spectrum of 4 % CDs/FeP before and after reaction. The peaks of Fe, P, C, and O elements of 4 % CDs/FeP match before and after the reaction which testified to the chemical stability of the catalyst. Fig. S8 and Fig. S9 exhibit the TEM and SEM images of 4 % CDs/FeP after reaction, confirming the stability of the catalyst. The XRD pattern of 4 % CDs/FeP shown in Fig. S10 matches JCPDS-No. 78–1443, indicating that FeP in 4 % CDs/FeP remained after reaction. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of 4 % CDs/FeP is exhibited in Fig. 4d, which shows that 4 % CDs/FeP has a maximum AQE value of 0.703 ‰ at the wavelength of 500 nm, and the detailed calculation is shown in S2 Part, Supporting Information.

Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and transient photocurrents response of pristine FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP were tested to explore the photocatalytic activity of 4 % CDs/FeP. Fig. 5 a shows the EIS result of both materials. The fact that the arc radius, which indicates the performance of the charge delivery, of 4 % CDs/FeP is smaller than that of the pristine FeP, meaning 4 % CDs/FeP has lower transfer resistance and larger interfacial charge delivery rate [50]. The transient photocurrents response of FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP are shown in Fig. 5b, where the lamp was turned on and off five times. The result shows that 4 % CDs/FeP has a higher intensity of photocurrent response and reveals that the addition of CDs enhanced the split of photoinduced carriers.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test was carried on to further understand the water oxidation reaction (WOR) in a rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) electrode system in N2 saturation (0.1 M phosphate buffer solution) [51]. To detect O2 evolution in the reaction, a potential of −0.23 V vs. Ag/AgCl was applied to the ring electrode and the potential of the disk was applied to scan from 1.7 V to 2.3 V (vs. RHE) by 10 mV/s. As the ring-disk electrode rotates at a high speed (1600 rpm), the O2 produced at the disk electrode spreads quickly to the ring electrode and is reduced at the ring electrode. The disk current in Fig. 5c shows a rapid increase after 1.9 V (vs. REH), which indicates that WOR occurs at the disk electrode for 4 % CDs/FeP. The ring current shows that O2 is generated during the WOR reaction.

Fig. 5d exhibits the i-t curve using an RRDE as the working electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode under N2 saturation (0.1 M Na2SO4) from dark to light on. The electron transfer number (n) of 4 % CDs/FeP was calculated from Fig. 6 d to be 3.39, which indicates that the photoinduced water decomposition reaction catalysed by 4 % CDs/FeP follows a four-electron route (S3 Part, Supporting Information, shown the detailed calculation). In conclusion, the water splitting reaction catalyzed by 4 % CDs/FeP proceeds via a four-electron step and oxidize water into O2.

The TPV measurements were used to explore the interfacial charge delivery kinetics of the FeP/CDs catalyst. Fig. 6a is the original TPV curve of FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP. The curve shows that 4 % CDs/FeP has greater photocurrent intensity than FeP. Fig. 6b shows the electron attenuation constants (τ) in the charge recombination process of FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP, which can evaluate the photocatalytic properties of the photocatalyst. As τ of 4 % CDs/FeP (τ2 = 0.6091 ms) is smaller than that of FeP (τ1 = 1.041 ms), which suggests that the addition of CDs improves the electron delivery rate. The integral area (A) of the TPV curve was shown in Fig. 6c, which represented the maximum photocarrier extraction efficiency of the photocatalyst. 4 % CDs/FeP (A2, 0.0981) has a larger A value than that of FeP (A1, 0.0354), indicating that the photocatalytic performance of 4 % CDs/FeP to extract charges is better than that of FeP. Fig. 6d exhibits the maximum charge transfer rate (tmax) of FeP and 4 % CDs/FeP which represents the time interval of the charge extraction process. It shows that the photocarrier extraction process of FeP (tmax1 = 0.7977 ms) is smaller than that of 4 % CDs/FeP (tmax2 = 0.6961 ms), which indicates that 4 % CDs/FeP has a larger maximum electron delivery rate. The TPV tests show that 4 % CDs/FeP has better photocatalytic performance to extract charges and has a larger maximum electron delivery rate over FeP. The above evidence proves that CDs have the ability to stabilize and store photogenerated electrons. To further explore the photocatalytic ability of the CDs/FeP catalyst, the surface effective electron numbers of 4 % CDs/FeP and FeP were calculated by the formula (ne = A*τ/tmax) and listed in Table S1. It exhibits that the ne of 4 % CDs/FeP (ne = 0.0858) has a literal improvement compared with that of the pristine FeP (ne = 0.0462), proving that 4 % CDs/FeP has a better photocatalytic ability [52].

Considering the aforementioned findings, a conceivable mechanism is put forward to elucidate the improved efficiency observed in the CDs/FeP system for photocatalytic hydrogen production, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . Upon exposure to light irradiation, the excitation of FeP generates photogenerated electrons and holes. Notably, the presence of CDs in the system enhances the electron sink effect, facilitating the efficient transfer of photogenerated electrons from FeP to the CDs. This electron transfer process subsequently drives the surface photoreduction of H+ on the CDs surface, leading to the generation of H2. Simultaneously, the photogenerated holes participate in the oxidation of H2O, resulting in the release of O2. It is worth mentioning that the electron-withdrawing nature of CDs plays a crucial role in the overall photocatalytic process. Consequently, the incorporation of 4 % CDs in the FeP catalyst demonstrates exceptional performance in terms of photocatalytic hydrogen production.

In this work, a CDs/FeP composite photocatalyst was fabricated, which can produce H2 through a typical 4e- pathway. Notably, the optimized 4 % CDs/FeP photocatalyst demonstrated an outstanding photocatalytic performance as the yield of H2 in pure water at room temperature and normal pressure without any sacrificial agents reaches 33.85 μmol/h/g, which is 5 times greater than that of the pristine FeP. Moreover, the TPV tests indicates that the addition of CDs improves the electron delivery rate, the ability to extract charges and the maximum electron delivery rate of FeP. These three factors together contribute to the photocatalyst performance of CDs/FeP. This work provides a new perspective on enhancing the photocatalytic water activity of phosphides by improving electron transfer at the phosphide interface.

Honglin Si: Investigation. Zenan Li: Formal analysis. Tianyang Zhang: Methodology. Zhenglong Fan: Software. Hui Huang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yang Liu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20220028), National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), National MCF Energy R&D Program of China (2018YFE0306105), National Natural Science Foundation of China (52271223, ‬52272043, 52202107, 52201269, 51821002), The Science and Technology Development Fund, Macau SAR (0009/2022/ITP), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.159153.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
Supplementary Information available: The size distribution of CDs/FeP, XRD patterns, XPS survey spectra, characterization after cycling, schematic of synthetic route.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advanced nanoarchitectures for solar photocatalytic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kubacka, M. Fernandez-Garcia, G. Colon, Advanced nanoarchitectures for solar photocatalytic applications, Chem. Rev., 112 (2012) 1555-1614. https://10.1021/cr100454n.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heterogeneous photocatalyst materials for water splitting
